nativeRange.habitat: Slow-flowing minor tributaries, backwaters, and oxbow lakes in the Araguaia drainage; soft, acidic water with leaf litter, submerged wood, and dense aquatic vegetation

diet.notes: Micropredator by nature; readily accepts high-quality micro-pellets, crushed flakes, and nano-sized prepared foods. Benefits from regular small live or frozen foods such as baby brine shrimp, Daphnia, Moina, and micro-worms to enhance coloration and condition.

imageCandidates.notes: Cedricguppy - Loury Cedric; 1024x577; two individuals, sharp focus, vibrant fiery orange-red coloration clearly showing the species' diagnostic color.
Joel Carnat; 1024x777; a school of ~5 embers in a planted blackwater-style tank, illustrating the schooling behavior; slight green haze but orange color visible.
Joel Carnat; 1024x776; single individual in profile, sharp on the fish showing translucent orange body form; coloration somewhat muted and minor watermark text.

summary: The Ember Tetra is a tiny, fiery orange-red fish from the slow-moving waters of the Araguaia basin in Brazil. At under 2 cm, it is well suited to nano aquariums and planted tanks, where dark substrates tend to bring out its color. Generally considered peaceful and safe with adult dwarf shrimp.
careNotes: Ember Tetras tend to show their best coloration — a bright fiery orange-red — when kept in well-planted tanks with dark substrates and subdued lighting that mimics their blackwater origin. A school of 8 or more is generally advised for natural schooling behavior and full color display; smaller groups have been known to be more subdued and skittish. They are generally safe companions for adult dwarf shrimp (Neocaridina, Caridina), though fry and very small juveniles may be at risk. Gentle filtration is recommended — strong flow has been reported to stress them. Avoid keeping with boisterous or substantially larger tankmates. Water quality stability tends to matter more than hitting precise parameter targets within the recommended range.
breedingNotes: The Ember Tetra is an egg-scattering free-spawner that provides no parental care. Spawning is generally achievable in a dedicated 10–20 L tank fitted with fine-leaf plants or a mesh over the substrate to protect the eggs from falling into areas where adults can reach them. Dimly lit conditions, soft slightly acidic water (pH around 6.5–7.0), and temperatures toward the upper end of their range (26–28°C) are commonly recommended. Conditioning the adult fish on live or frozen foods for roughly two weeks beforehand has been reported to improve spawn success. Eggs typically hatch within 24–48 hours, and the fry initially require infusoria or very fine powdered food before graduating to baby brine shrimp nauplii. Adults should be removed from the spawning tank promptly, as they will consume eggs and fry.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked taxonomy, native range, water parameters, minimum tank dimensions, grouping, and breeding behavior. gH given as 18–179 ppm (≈1–10 dGH); temperature range 20–28°C confirmed.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (10 gal / ~38L), lifespan (up to 2 years), pH and temperature. Lifespan averaged with ModestFish 2–4 year figure.

additional.name: ModestFish
additional.notes: Confirmed kH 4–8, gH 5–17 dGH (upper end), lifespan 2–4 years, temperature 23–29°C. Values averaged across all four sources for final figures.
